title: Lane Chains
desc: At Queen's Gate, traffic only feels natural because the lane posts keep it from becoming chaos.

steps.text:
The lanes at Queen's Gate are not decorative. Chain runs separate incoming workers from outbound patrols, cart traffic from courier traffic, and creatures with proper paperwork from creatures who are about to have a worse morning than expected. When the lane posts slip or the chains sag, the crowd notices by becoming itself more honestly than anyone at the gate prefers.

The traffic marshal points at a row of leaning posts and one chain run that now hangs more like an invitation than a barrier. "Retension the main lane if you want the heavier work," she says. "Or reset the side-channel posts before the next cart train decides geometry is optional."

options.label: Retension the main traffic chain along the arch
Reset the side-lane posts for cart clearance

steps.text:
The main lane chain takes the brunt of everyday impatience. It gets leaned on, shoved aside, ducked under, and occasionally tested by creatures who seem offended by the existence of boundaries. Today one run has stretched and another has lost a hook clasp, enough to let the whole queue bow outward where it should be moving straight. You draw the slack out link by link, reset the clasp, and bring the run back into a firm guiding curve that says move here and nowhere else.

When the marshal sends a test line through, the effect is immediate. The queue stops spilling into the courier lane. The recognition stones stop being approached from three angles at once. The gate regains that particular form of order that looks effortless only because iron is doing part of the argument for everyone. The marshal grips the corrected chain and nods. "Good. Now the crowd can go back to pretending it chose to behave."

options.label: Hang the spare hooks away and leave the lane strict again

steps.text:
The side-channel posts are thicker and lower than the main lane runs because they guide loaded carts through the arch without letting wheels chew up the pedestrian blocks. Two have shifted from repeated side pressure and one has been clipped hard enough to cant the whole row. You reseat them, tamp the stone collars tight, and check the wheel clearances against the marshal's measuring rod until the side lane reads clean from curb to curb again.

The next supply cart that comes through does so without clipping a post or dragging the chain with it, which is the only praise the marshal was interested in receiving today. She watches the wheels clear the corrected line and says, "There. Let the carts complain about the slope instead." At Queen's Gate, complaint is unavoidable. Redirecting it counts as success.

options.label: Brush the stone collars smooth and reopen the side channel
